summary:
The Trialetian culture was an Epipaleolithic hunter-gatherer tradition of the South Caucasus region — encompassing modern Georgia, Armenia, Azerbaijan, and adjacent parts of Turkey and Iran — dated to approximately 15,000–10,000 BCE. Named after the Trialeti region of southern Georgia, where several characteristic sites have been excavated, the culture is identified by a distinctive blade-based stone tool industry with geometric microliths and backed bladelets.

Trialetian communities inhabited the diverse ecological zones of the South Caucasus — mountain forests, river valleys, sub-alpine meadows, and the steppe margins — exploiting a wide range of game animals, plant foods, and aquatic resources. The Caucasus served as a corridor between the Near East to the south and the Eurasian steppes to the north, and Trialetian communities were positioned at the interface of cultural influences from both directions. The South Caucasus lies at the periphery of the early Neolithic zone centered in the Levant and southeastern Anatolia, and while it may have participated in some interactions with early agricultural communities, the full Neolithic transition came later to the Caucasus than to the Fertile Crescent heartland.
